title: Understanding High Blood Pressure (Hypertension)
content: High blood pressure often has no obvious symptoms, which is why it is sometimes called a 'silent' condition -- many people only find out through a check-up. When symptoms do occur, they can include headaches, dizziness, or blurred vision, but the only reliable way to know is to have blood pressure checked regularly, ideally at least once a year for adults, more often if already diagnosed. Reducing salt intake, staying physically active, maintaining a healthy weight, and limiting alcohol all help manage blood pressure alongside any medication a health worker prescribes.

title: When High Blood Pressure Becomes an Emergency
content: A hypertensive emergency should be treated urgently if severe headache, chest pain, difficulty breathing, vision changes, confusion, or weakness on one side of the body occur alongside known high blood pressure -- these can be signs of a stroke or heart complication and need immediate medical attention.

title: Understanding Diabetes
content: Diabetes means the body has trouble controlling blood sugar levels. Common warning signs include excessive thirst, frequent urination, unexplained weight loss, constant hunger, blurred vision, and slow-healing wounds or cuts. Diabetes is diagnosed through a blood test at a health facility, not by symptoms alone -- anyone noticing these signs should get tested rather than guessing. Managing diabetes generally involves a balanced diet lower in sugar and refined carbohydrates, regular physical activity, and consistent medical follow-up.

title: Diabetes Emergency Warning Signs
content: A person with diabetes needs urgent care if they become confused, unusually drowsy, difficult to wake, have very rapid breathing, or lose consciousness -- these can be signs of dangerously high or low blood sugar. If a diabetic person becomes confused or faints and a fast-acting sugar source (like juice or sugar water) is available, offering it while arranging urgent transport to a health facility can help, but this does not replace emergency medical care.
